elements.sent-2411-001: We study an extension to directed graphs of the parameter called the $b$-chromatic number of a graph in terms of acyclic vertex colorings: the dib-chromatic number.
elements.sent-2411-002: We give general bounds for this parameter.
elements.sent-2411-003: We also show some results about tournaments and regular digraphs.
elements.sent-2411-004: Complete coloring, acyclic coloring, b-chromatic number, Nordhaus-Gaddum relations, directed graph.. Mathematics Subject Classification.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this paper, we introduce the concept of the dib-chromatic number of a digraph, which is a generalization of the b-chromatic number of a graph.
elements.sent-2411-006: The term $b$-chromatic number of a digraph is reserved for the $b$-chromatic number of the underlying graph of a given digraph; see \cite{MR3511873 .}
elements.sent-2411-007: To begin with, we recall concepts about graphs.
elements.sent-2411-008: A coloring of a graph $G$ is proper if each color class induces an empty subgraph.
elements.sent-2411-009: The chromatic number $\chi(G)$ of $G$ is the smallest natural number $k$ such that $G$ admits a proper coloring with $k$ colors.
elements.sent-2411-010: A $b$-coloring of a graph $G$ with $k$ colors is a coloring of the vertices of $G$ such that in each color class there exists a vertex having neighbors in all the other $k-1$ color classes.
elements.sent-2411-011: Such a vertex will be called a $b$-vertex.
elements.sent-2411-012: The $b$-chromatic number $b(G)$ of a graph $G$ is the largest $k$ for which $G$ has a proper $b$-coloring with $k$ colors \cite{MR1670155 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: This parameter belongs to a family of parameters that comes from complete colorings, namely, the chromatic number, the Grundy number, and the achromatic number (and their no-proper versions).
elements.sent-2411-014: A coloring of a graph $G$ is complete if for every pair of colors $i$ and $j$ there exists an edge $uv$ such that $u$ is colored $i$ and $v$ is colored $j$.
elements.sent-2411-015: In fact, any proper coloring of $G$ with $\chi(G)$ colors is complete.
elements.sent-2411-016: Another way to understand this parameter comes from algorithmic graph theory.
elements.sent-2411-017: Suppose a graph with a given proper coloring.
elements.sent-2411-018: We would like to perform an operation to reduce the number of colors as follows.
elements.sent-2411-019: Recolor its vertices, but not necessarily each one, with the same color.
elements.sent-2411-020: Clearly, such recoloring is not possible if each color class contains a $b$-vertex.
elements.sent-2411-021: Hence, the $b$-chromatic number of the graph serves as the worst case for the number of colors used by this coloring heuristic method.
elements.sent-2411-022: Next, we define digraph, the dib-chromatic number of a digraph, and we recall some valuable results about the digrundy and diachromatic numbers.
elements.sent-2411-023: In this paper, we consider simple, loopless, and finite digraphs.
elements.sent-2411-024: A digraph $D$ is a pair $(V,A)$ where $V$ is the set of vertices and $A$ is the set of darts.
elements.sent-2411-025: A dart (or arc) is a pair $(u,v)$ of vertices, for short we write $uv$, such that $u\not=v$.
elements.sent-2411-026: A coloring of a digraph $D$ is acyclic if each color class induces a subdigraph without directed cycles.
elements.sent-2411-027: The dichromatic number $dc(D)$ of a digraph $D$ is the smallest number $k$ such that $D$ admits an acyclic coloring with $k$ colors \cite{MR693366 .}
elements.sent-2411-028: A coloring of a digraph $D$ is complete if for every pair of different colors $(i,j)$ there exists at least one dart $uv$ such that $u$ is colored $i$ and $v$ is colored $j$ \cite{MR3329642 (see also \cite{MR2998438,MR2895432 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Any acyclic coloring of $D$ with $dc(D)$ colors is also a complete coloring.
elements.sent-2411-030: The diachromatic number $dac(D)$ of $D$ is the largest number of colors for which there exists a complete and acyclic coloring of $D$ \cite{MR3875016 .}
elements.sent-2411-031: Let $u$ and $v$ be two vertices of a digraph $D$ such that $f=uv$ is a dart of $D$.
elements.sent-2411-032: We say that $f$ is incident from $u$, and incident to $v$, while $u$ is incident to $f$ and $v$ is incident from $f$.
elements.sent-2411-033: The out-degree $deg ^+(u)$ of a vertex $u$ is the number of darts that are incident from $u$.
elements.sent-2411-034: Similarly, the in-degree $deg^-(v)$ of a vertex $v$ is the number of darts incident to $v$.
elements.sent-2411-035: The degree $deg(v)$ of a vertex $v$ is defined by $deg(v)=deg^+(v)+ deg^-(v)$.
elements.sent-2411-036: We also say that $u$ is incident to $v$ and $v$ is incident from $u$.
elements.sent-2411-037: A vertex $u$ is called a $b^+$-vertex with respect to a coloring $\varsigma$ of a digraph $D$ if $u$ is incident to a vertex colored with each color different from the color of $u$.
elements.sent-2411-038: Analogously, a vertex $v$ is a $b^-$-vertex with respect to $\varsigma$ if $v$ is incident from a vertex colored with each color different from the color of $v$.
elements.sent-2411-039: A $b$-coloring of $D$ is a coloring of $D$ such that each color class contains a $b^+$-vertex and a $b^-$-vertex.
elements.sent-2411-040: We define the $dib$-chromatic number of $D$, denoted by $dib(D)$, as the largest $k$ such that $D$ admits an acyclic $b$-coloring with $k$ colors.
elements.sent-2411-041: For any digraph $D$, any $b$-coloring of $D$ is also a complete coloring, and \begin{equation}\label{eq1} dc(D)\leq dib(D)\leq dac(D).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-042: If $\Delta^+(D)$ denotes the maximum out-degree and $\Delta^-(D)$ denotes the maximum in-degree, then $k \leq \Delta(D)+1$ for any $b$-coloring of $D$ using $k$ colors where $\Delta(D)=\min\{\Delta^+(D),\Delta^-(D)\}$ ( or simply by $\Delta$, $\Delta^+$ and $\Delta^-$ when the digraph $D$ is understood).. In particular, \begin{equation}\label{eq2} dib(D) \leq \Delta+1.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-043: Moreover, for an induced subdigraph $H$ of a digraph $D$, \begin{equation}\label{eq3} dib(H) \leq dib(D).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-044: A dart $uv\in A(D)$ is symmetric if $vu\in A(D)$ and asymmetric if $vu\notin A(D)$.
elements.sent-2411-045: Two vertices $u,v$ are adjacent if the dart $uv$ is symmetric.
elements.sent-2411-047: If $D$ is a symmetric digraph, it can be considered as a graph, and then $dib(D)=b(D)$.
elements.sent-2411-048: For example, complete symmetric digraph of order $n$, denoted by $K_n$, has both darts $(u,v)$ and $(v,u)$ for all two distinct vertices $u$ and $v$.
elements.sent-2411-050: This paper is organized into three sections: The second section contains general bounds such as the Nordhaus-Gaddum relations.
elements.sent-2411-051: The third section deals with tournaments and fourth section with the dib-chromatic number in regular digraphs.
elements.sent-2411-052: A clique of a digraph $D$ is an induced subdigraph of $D$ which is a complete symmetric subdigraph.
elements.sent-2411-053: A maximum clique of $D$ is a clique such that there is no clique with more vertices, and the clique number $\omega(D)$ of $D$ is the number of vertices in a maximum clique in $D$.. Then $\omega(D)\leq dc(D)$.
elements.sent-2411-054: Let $D$ be a digraph of $n$ vertices that are listed in some specified order.
elements.sent-2411-055: In a greedy coloring of $D$, the vertices are colored with positive integers according to a greedy algorithm that assigns to the vertex under consideration the smallest available color.
elements.sent-2411-056: Therefore, a greedy coloring is also a complete coloring, see \cite{MR3875016,MR4426060 .}
elements.sent-2411-057: It is not hard to see that a greedy acyclic coloring of a graph $D$ using $k$ colors is a coloring of $D$ having the property that for every two colors $i$ and $j$ with $i<j$, every vertex colored $j$ has a neighbor colored $i$.
elements.sent-2411-058: Note that for each vertex $v$ in the color class $V_j$ and each color class $V_i$, with $i<j$, $N^+(v)\cap V_i\not=\emptyset$ and $N^-(v)\cap V_i\not=\emptyset$ then $k \leq \Delta(D)+1$, see also \cite{MR4426060 .}
elements.sent-2411-059: Let $\varsigma$ be a $b$-coloring of a digraph $D$ using $k$ colors.
elements.sent-2411-060: On the one hand, let $u_i$ be any $b^+$-vertex of color $i$; we say that the set $\{u_1,\dots,u_k\}$ is a positive basis of $\varsigma$.
elements.sent-2411-061: On the other hand, let $v_i$ be any $b^-$-vertex colored $i$; we say that the set $\{v_1,\dots,v_k\}$ is a negative basis of $\varsigma$.
elements.sent-2411-062: A $b$-coloring may have many positive and negative bases, and the intersection between positive and negative bases can be empty or not.
elements.sent-2411-063: The following result is a generalization of a result in \cite{MR1927071 .}

elements.sent-2411-083: An independent set of vertices in a digraph $D$ is a set $I$ of vertices such that for every two vertices in $I$, there is no dart connecting the two.

elements.sent-2411-137: A digraph is strongly connected if for every pair of vertices $u$ and $v$ there is a directed $uv$-path.
elements.sent-2411-138: The strongly connected components of a digraph $D$ induced equivalence relation $\sim$ into subdigraphs that are themselves strongly connected, therefore we have the digraph $\tilde{D := D/\sim$.}
elements.sent-2411-139: If $T$ is a tournament with (exactly) $k$ strongly connected components, then $k/2\leq dib(T)$.
elements.sent-2411-141: The digraph $D$ is $H$-free if $D$ has no subdigraph isomorphic to $H$.
elements.sent-2411-142: A tournament $H$ is a hero if and only if there exists $\alpha>0$ such that every $H$-free tournament $D$ has a transitive subset of cardinality at least $\alpha |V (D)|$, see \cite{MR2995716 .}
elements.sent-2411-143: If $H$ is a hero and $D$ is an $H$-free tournament, then $dib(D)=\Theta(n)$.

elements.sent-2411-155: A digraph $D$ is called $r$-regular if $deg^+(v)=deg^-(v)=r$ for every vertex $v\in D$.

elements.sent-2411-172: We conjecture that the remaining $2$-regular digraphs have dib-chromatic number 3.
elements.sent-2411-173: Another nice problem could be to generate the set of digraphs of small order with vertices of small degrees (with digons).
